data.title: Conagra targets 105% free cash flow conversion while entering fiscal '27 with ~60% material coverage in Q1
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Conagra Brands (CAG) Q3 fiscal 2026

Management View

  • CEO Sean Connolly said Conagra’s pivot “at the beginning of our fiscal '24” to restore volume growth in frozen and snacks “has proven to be quite effective,” adding that “our volume trajectory improve[d] every quarter since” except for a period of “temporary supply constraints,” and that the company was “pleased to see our total portfolio growing again this quarter.” (President, CEO & Director Sean Connolly)
  • Connolly emphasized flexibility on inflation and pricing, saying, “our plan at this point is to stay agile,” and added the company is “intensely focused on maximizing cash flow,” while also pointing to pricing already taken in “our canned foods and our cocoa-oriented products” where “the elasticities have been quite encouraging.” (President, CEO & Director Connolly)
  • CFO Dave Marberger provided early cost-positioning detail for fiscal ’27, stating, “we're roughly 60% covered… for Q1 and roughly 40% covered for the full fiscal year,” with more coverage in “steel, freight… and then some of our crop-based ingredients,” less in “diesel fuel,” and “proteins probably have the lowest coverage… we're only about 15% covered.” (Executive VP & CFO David Marberger)

Outlook

  • Marberger reiterated Q4 organic growth expectations, saying, “we do expect positive organic net sales growth in Q4,” and tied it to the full-year guide, adding this is “implied with our full year guide to the kind of the midpoint of the range for organic.” (Executive VP & CFO Marberger)
  • On margin cadence into Q4, Marberger said, “we expect an inflection from Q3 to Q4,” citing that “A&P as a percentage of sales will not be as high in Q4,” the “53rd week actually gives some leverage,” plus “seasonality of trade, timing of productivity, timing of inflation.” (Executive VP & CFO Marberger)
  • On the full-year operating margin range, Marberger said, “we're actually now going to guide to the higher end of that range,” referencing the initial “11% to 11.5% operating margin.” (Executive VP & CFO Marberger)
  • Compared with Q2’s call, management’s external posture shifted from second-half organic sales growth expectations to explicit Q4 expectations; in Q2 Connolly said, “we do expect organic net sales growth in the second half,” while in Q3 Marberger stated, “we do expect positive organic net sales growth in Q4.” (President, CEO & Director Sean Connolly; Executive VP & CFO Marberger)

Financial Results

  • The transcript did not include explicit quarterly revenue, EPS, or net income figures, and it did not disclose the updated full-year organic net sales range or the specific operating margin point within the range.
  • Marberger highlighted an updated cash conversion target, saying, “we took our conversion up to 105% from 100%, and we took it up at CAGNY,” and added that “free cash flow is in their incentive” across the company. (Executive VP & CFO Marberger)
  • Marberger described inventory as a major balance sheet lever, stating, “we have $2 billion of inventory,” and added, “with Project Catalyst and us being able to leverage AI and other technology, we think we have a long runway to keep taking inventory out and be more competitive.” (Executive VP & CFO Marberger)
  • On inflation components discussed in Q&A, Marberger reiterated the fiscal ’26 framework: “our overall inflation was 7%, 4% was core and 3% were gross tariffs before mitigation,” and said it was “still at that kind of total 7%.” (Executive VP & CFO Marberger)

Q&A

  • Andrew Lazar, Barclays: asked whether Conagra could rely on pricing if inflation returns while consumers are “particularly value conscious”; President, CEO & Director Connolly said Conagra will “stay agile,” may keep focusing on “volume momentum” if inflation is “benign,” but if inflation rises it will “keep our options open,” while driving “a lot of productivity” and noting pricing elasticities were “quite encouraging.”
  • Andrew Lazar, Barclays: asked about fiscal ’27 cost visibility via hedges; Executive VP & CFO Marberger said fiscal ’27 coverage is “roughly 60%… for Q1 and roughly 40%… for the full fiscal year,” with proteins at “about 15% covered.”
  • Megan Christine Alexander, Morgan Stanley: asked about Q4 building blocks and shipments vs. consumption; Connolly said not to “overthink shipments versus consumption,” adding that over fiscal ’25 and ’26 combined Conagra is “shipping almost exactly to consumption,” while Marberger said “we do expect positive organic net sales growth in Q4” and that “consumption and shipments should be more in line in Q4.”
  • Megan Christine Alexander, Morgan Stanley: asked what’s driving the move to the high end of op margin guide and whether Q4 exit rate is a starting point; Marberger said, “I'm not going to comment on fiscal '27,” and attributed confidence to being “pretty much on” the inflation call, productivity “really doing well,” and price increases with elasticities “in line.”
  • Peter Galbo, BofA: asked about the Ardent Mills revision and Q4 dynamics; Connolly said Ardent has “core business margin” and “commodity trading revenue,” and noted “wheat prices have been low and there's been less volatility,” while “with the start of the war, wheat prices have gone up… and volatility has increased,” but “we don't have line of sight” for next year.
  • Thomas Palmer, JPMorgan: asked about freight contracts holding amid spot spikes; Marberger said spot “has now spiked up,” but “a high percentage of our freight… is contracted line haul,” and Conagra has “incorporated all that for our fiscal '26 guide.”
  • Robert Moskow, TD Cowen: asked about tariff inflation and fiscal ’27 lapping; Marberger said fiscal ’26 tariffs were “3%… before mitigation,” and for next year there will be a “headwind… just because we're wrapping on the mitigation,” adding it “might be more like half” of the originally implied “$80 million.”
  • Christopher Carey, Wells Fargo: asked about multi-year margin recovery in key U.S. businesses; Connolly said margin compression in frozen reflected a choice to “eat some of that higher cost,” particularly “animal protein,” and reiterated margin expansion building blocks including productivity, supply chain resiliency investments, price actions, and Project Catalyst.
  • Scott Marks, Jefferies: asked about the chicken facility and repatriation; Connolly said baked and fried chicken projects are “tracking right where we need them to be,” and Marberger added, “we did complete that project, and we're starting to bring that volume back this year,” with “a tailwind” next year from a full-year benefit, while “the fried… is going to go out longer.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to skeptical on inflation, elasticity, tariffs, freight volatility, and Ardent Mills visibility, including questions that pressed on whether “this time is different” for pricing, and whether tariff effects “lap” into fiscal ’27. (Andrew Lazar, Barclays; Robert Moskow, TD Cowen)
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared responses but cautious on forecasting, repeatedly emphasizing agility and refusing to pre-guide fiscal ’27; Connolly said “it's too early to speculate,” and Marberger said, “I'm not going to comment on fiscal '27.”
  • Versus Q2, management’s tone stayed cautious on macro visibility; Q2 included Connolly’s statement, “I'm more cautious on prognosticating about inflation now than I probably have ever been,” while Q3 continued with “we'll be agile” and “too early to speculate.” (President, CEO & Director Connolly)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The core strategic framing remained consistent: Q3 repeated the “horses for courses strategy” and the fiscal ’24 pivot toward volume in “frozen and snacks,” while Q2 emphasized that Conagra “did not take pricing… we kept pricing where it was” to support volume inflection.
  • New in Q3 was more granular fiscal ’27 commodity coverage disclosure (materials, freight, proteins), while Q2 focused more on Project Catalyst’s concept and future disclosure cadence: “during calendar '26, we'll unpack this in more detail.” (President, CEO & Director Connolly)
  • Ardent Mills remained a recurring investor focus in both quarters; Q2 discussed an Ardent-related shortfall bridge inside EPS range, while Q3 centered on wheat volatility and war-driven futures moves and reiterated limited near-term visibility.

Risks and Concerns

  • Connolly described a “war curveball” affecting inflation uncertainty and wheat volatility, noting Ardent’s commodity trading upside depends on “overall wheat prices and the volatility of the markets.” (President, CEO & Director Connolly)
  • Marberger flagged fiscal ’27 exposure in proteins due to low hedge coverage: “we're only about 15% covered.” (Executive VP & CFO Marberger)
  • Management cited freight spot volatility as a risk factor, with Marberger stating, “spot has now spiked up,” while emphasizing that “a high percentage” remains under contract. (Executive VP & CFO Marberger)

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized that Conagra is prioritizing volume momentum in frozen and snacks while keeping the ability to pivot if inflation re-accelerates, with Connolly repeatedly framing the approach as “agile.” Marberger pointed investors to early fiscal ’27 cost visibility through commodity coverage, an expectation for Q4 organic sales growth and a Q3-to-Q4 operating margin inflection, and a stronger free cash flow posture anchored by a higher conversion target and a stated focus on inventory reduction supported by Project Catalyst and AI-enabled process changes.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Healthcare among laggards in Q1; MRNA and DVA lead gainers while HUM drags
data.description:
The Health Care Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLV) declined 5.29% in the first quarter of 2026; it slightly underperformed the broader S&P 500 Index, which fell 4.63% over the same period. Market sentiment suffered due to persistent inflation, rising Treasury yields, and an oil supply shock, pushing Brent prices over $100, peaking near $118 in late March, which hurt the healthcare sector's performance.

Performance across the 11 S&P 500 sectors has diverged notably, reflecting shifting sentiment amid geopolitical uncertainty. In the first quarter of 2026, the Energy Select Sector (XLE) increased by 37.02% year-to-date, outpacing the broader market, which saw the S&P 500 Index fall by 4.63%. While defensive sectors like Materials (XLB) and Utilities (XLU) remained positive. In contrast, the former leaders of the bull market—Technology (XLK) and Financials (XLF)—are entering Q2 deeply in the red.

The top S&P 500 healthcare gainers and losers for Q1 2026 are as follows:

Q1 top performers:

Moderna (MRNA) +63.55%

DaVita (DVA) +34.12%

Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) +17.17%

Quest Diagnostics (DGX) +13.62%

Merck & Co. (MRK) +12.20%

Q1 bottom performers:

Humana (HUM) -35.19%

Boston Scientific (BSX) -34.00%

Insulet Corporation (PODD) -26.82%

IQVIA Holdings (IQV) -26.29%

Waters Corporation (WAT) -22.76%

Industries performance:

The Health Care Equipment & Services sector declined 12.32% in Q1, while Pharmaceuticals, Biotechnology and Life Sciences are down 13.85% in Q1.

What Quantitative Measures Say

The Health Care Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLV) has seen a severe downgrade in its Seeking Alpha Quant Rating, plunging from a 'Buy' (3.59/5) to a 'Sell' (1.3/5). This regression was driven by a dramatic collapse in momentum—which fell from a B to a D grade—even as liquidity remained a bright spot at A+. Meanwhile, the ETF's risk profile rating shifted slightly from D to D+.

What Analysts Expect

Investors can buy XLV or similar index funds like Vanguard’s VHT to get exposure to the healthcare sector. For those willing to take on more risk, RSPH is an equal-weighted option that offers more access to smaller companies, which can yield higher returns," said Seeking Alpha analyst Envision Research.

"For those seeking diversification, the Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF is a good option, with healthcare among its top exposures, likely increasing after its 2026 reconstitution. Investors may also look at individual stocks like JNJ, ABBV, and MRK," said the analyst.

On average, Seeking Alpha contributors see XLV as Hold.

Fund Flows

Despite a challenging quarterly return of -5.29%, investor conviction in the healthcare sector remained surprisingly resilient throughout the first three months of 2026. According to the latest flow data, the XLV ETF recorded net inflows of $1.11 billion between January 1 and March 30.

data.title: AI, chip stocks soar on hopes of resolution of Iran war
data.description:
Chip and AI-related stocks climbed on Wednesday amid hopes of a potential resolution to the Iran war.

U.S. President Donald Trump will deliver a prime-time address on Wednesday at 9 p.m. EST to provide an update on the war in Iran. Separately, Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, or IRGC, has reportedly threatened attacks on several U.S. technology companies operating in the Middle East, including Nvidia (NVDA), Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), and Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG).

The tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) jumped about 1.4%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) rose about 1%. The blue-chip Dow (DJI) climbed nearly 0.9%.

Shares of AI chipmaker Nvidia (NVDA) rose about 1%, while Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) surged nearly 4%. Broadcom (AVGO) and Qualcomm (QCOM) each climbed nearly 1%.

Several other AI and networking-related stocks were also among the gainers. Lumentum (LITE) surged around 11%, while Applied Optoelectronics (AAOI) and Coherent (COHR) each soared about 8%. Ciena (CIEN) jumped nearly 7%, while Corning (GLW) grew about 6%. Celestica (CLS) jumped about 5%, Arista Networks (ANET) climbed around 3%, and Cisco (CSCO) rose about 2%.

Micron Technology (MU) soared about 11%, while Intel (INTC) — which announced that it is buying back a 49% equity interest in a joint venture related to its Fab 34 facility in Ireland from Apollo for $14.2B —  surged nearly 10%. Marvell Technology (MRVL) jumped about 7%, and  Lattice Semiconductor (LSCC) grew nearly 4%. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) rose about 3%, Analog Devices (ADI) climbed around 2%, while Arm (ARM), GlobalFoundries (GFS), and Texas Instruments (TXN) each gained nearly 1%. 

Chip equipment makers: ASML (ASML), Applied Materials (AMAT), and Lam Research (LRCX) each surged about 5%, while KLA (KLAC) soared nearly 4%.
